risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were randomly allocated in a 1:1 ratio to receive either Pain Exposure Physical Therapy... or conventional guideline-based treatment." Comment: The paper states that participants were randomly allocated in a 1:1 ratio, which indicates random sequence generation. Although the exact randomization mechanism is not described in this excerpt, the wording is sufficient, under the permissive criteria, to judge low risk because it clearly reports randomized allocation with no evidence of a non-random or predictable method.

Summary: The report states only that patients were "randomly allocated in a 1:1 ratio" to the two treatments, but it gives no description of how the allocation was concealed from the recruiter/enrolling person. There is no explicit mention of central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, identical drug containers, or any other mechanism preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment. Comment: Random allocation alone does not establish concealment, so the risk of selection bias from allocation concealment cannot be judged as low.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The study is described as a "single-blind RCT," and the interventions were very different and clearly distinguishable (pain exposure physical therapy versus conventional guideline-based treatment with pharmacological/other therapy). The paper does not state that participants and treating personnel were successfully blinded, and the intervention content would make blinding of those delivering/receiving treatment implausible. Because outcomes included pain, disability, and quality of life, knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect them. Comment: This supports a high risk of performance bias across the study outcomes overall.

Summary: The study is described as a "single-blind RCT," but the methods do not explicitly state that outcome assessors were blinded. Outcome assessment involved a mix of patient-reported questionnaires (e.g. EQ-5D, Pain Disability Index, SF-36, DASH-DLV) and clinical measurements (ISS-RV components include pain, active range of motion, temperature; also muscle strength and performance tests). Because the paper does not specify who assessed these outcomes or whether assessors were blinded, and because several outcomes are subjective/judgement-dependent, detection bias cannot be ruled out. Comment: The absence of a direct statement about blinded outcome assessment makes the risk of bias unclear.

Quote: "Data on health-related quality of life were available for 42 patients (23 in the Pain Exposure Physical Therapy group and 19 in the conventional treatment group)." Comment: Although follow-up outcome data were incomplete for EQ-5D, the paper states that 56 patients were randomized and analyzed according to intention-to-treat, and the missing data were described as random in the discussion: "That is why cost data were available for only 48 out of the 56 patients. These data were missing at random... Therefore, we did not impute missing data and we only used the data available for our cost-effectiveness analysis." The attrition shown here is moderate and there is no clear signal that missingness was differential or related to outcome; the study also reports that most participants completed follow-up and the main analyses used ITT, so bias from incomplete outcome data is unlikely.
